volumeTitle: Trojan Women. Iphigenia among the Taurians. Ion
label: Trojan Women (Section 1.1071 - Section 1275)

sections.label: Section 1.1071
sections.translation: Gone are your sacrifices ! gone the dancer ’ s cheerful shout ! gone the vigils of the gods as night closed in ! your images of carven gold are now no more ;

sections.label: Section 1.1075
sections.translation: and Phrygia ’ s holy festivals , twelve times a year , at each full moon , are ended now . It is this , it is this that fills me with anxious thought whether you , lord , seated on the sky , your heavenly throne , care at all that my city is destroyed ,

sections.label: Section 1.1080
sections.translation: a prey to the furious fiery blast .

sections.label: Section 2.1081
sections.translation: Ah ! my loved husband , you are a wandering spectre ;

sections.label: Section 2.1085
sections.original: ἄθαπτος ἄνυδρος , ἐμὲ δὲ πόντιον σκάφος
sections.translation: unwashed , unburied lies your corpse , while over the sea the ship sped by wings will carry me to Argos , land of steeds , where stand Cyclopian walls of stone reaching to heaven . There in the gate the children gather ,

sections.label: Section 2.1090
sections.translation: and weep their piteous lamentation ; they cry , they cry : Mother , alas ! torn from your sight , the Achaeans bear me away from you to their dark ship

sections.label: Section 2.1095
sections.translation: to row me over the deep to sacred Salamis or to the hill on the Isthmus , that overlooks two seas , the seat that holds the gates of Pelops .

sections.label: Section 2.1100
sections.translation: Oh may the sacred blazing thunderbolt of the Aegean , hurled in might , smite the ship of Menelaus full in the middle , on its way in mid-sea ,

sections.label: Section 2.1105
sections.translation: since he is carrying me away in bitter sorrow from the shores of Ilium to be a slave in Hellas , while the daughter of Zeus still keeps her golden mirrors , delight of maidens ’ hearts .

sections.label: Section 2.1110
sections.original: μηδὲ γαῖάν ποτʼ ἔλθοι Λάκαιναν πατρῷ-
sections.translation: Never may he reach his home in Laconia or his father ’ s hearth and home , nor come to the town of Pitane [*] or the temple of the goddess [*] with the gates of bronze , having taken as his captive the one whose marriage brought disgrace on Hellas through its length and breadth and woful anguish

sections.label: Section 2.1115
sections.translation: on the streams of Simois !

sections.label: Section 1118
sections.translation: All me ! ah me ! new troubles fall on my country , to take the place of those that still are fresh ! Behold ,

sections.label: Section 1120
sections.translation: you hapless wives of Troy , the corpse of Astyanax , whom the Danaids have cruelly slain by hurling him from the battlements .

sections.label: Section 1123
sections.original: Ἑκάβη , νεὼς μὲν πίτυλος εἷς λελειμμένος
sections.translation: Hecuba , one ship alone delays its plashing oars ,

sections.label: Section 1125
sections.translation: and it is soon to sail to the shores of Phthia freighted with the remnant of the spoils of Achilles ’ son ; for Neoptolemus is already out at sea , having heard that new calamities have befallen Peleus , for Acastus , son of Pelias , has banished him from the realm . Therefore he is gone , too quick to indulge in any delay ,

sections.label: Section 1130
sections.original: φροῦδος , μετʼ αὐτοῦ δʼ Ἀνδρομάχη , πολλῶν ἐμοὶ
sections.translation: and with him goes Andromache , who drew many tears from me when she set out from the land , wailing her country and crying her farewell to Hector ’ s tomb . And she begged her master leave to bury this poor dead child of Hector

sections.label: Section 1135
sections.original: ψυχὴν ἀφῆκεν Ἕκτορος τοῦ σοῦ γόνος ·
sections.translation: who breathed his last when hurled from the turrets ; entreating too that he would not carry this shield , the terror of the Achaeans — this shield with plates of brass with which his father would gird himself — to the home of Peleus or to the same bridal bower where she , Andromache ,

sections.label: Section 1140
sections.original: μήτηρ νεκροῦ τοῦδʼ Ἀνδρομάχη , λύπας ὁρᾶν ,
sections.translation: the mother of this corpse , would be wed , a bitter sight to her , but let her bury the child in it instead of in a coffin of cedar or a tomb of stone , and to your hands commit the corpse that you may deck it with robes and garlands as best you can with your present means ;

sections.label: Section 1145
sections.original: ἐπεὶ βέβηκε , καὶ τὸ δεσπότου τάχος
sections.translation: for she is far away and her master ’ s haste prevented her from burying the child herself . So we , when you have decked the corpse , will heap the earth above and set upon it a spear ; but do you with your best speed perform your allotted task ;

sections.label: Section 1150
sections.original: ἑνὸς μὲν οὖν μόχθου σʼ ἀπαλλάξας ἔχω ·
sections.translation: one toil however I have already spared you , for I crossed Scamander ’ s stream and bathed the corpse and cleansed its wounds . But now I will go to dig a grave for hiin , that our united efforts

sections.label: Section 1155
sections.original: ἐς ἓν ξυνελθόντʼ οἴκαδʼ ὁρμήσῃ πλάτην .
sections.translation: shortening our task may speed our ship towards home . Exit Talthybius .

sections.label: Section 1156
sections.translation: Place the shield upon the ground , Hector ’ s shield so deftly rounded , a piteous sight , a bitter grief for me to see . O you Achaeans , more reason have you to boast of your prowess than your wisdom . Why have you in terror of this child

sections.label: Section 1160
sections.translation: been guilty of a murder never matched before ? Did you fear that some day he would rear again the fallen walls of Troy ? It seems then you were nothing after all , when , though Hector ’ s fortunes in the war were prosperous and he had ten thousand other arms to back him , we still were daily overmatched ; and yet , now that our city is taken and every Phrygian slain ,

sections.label: Section 1165
sections.original: βρέφος τοσόνδʼ ἐδείσατʼ · οὐκ αἰνῶ φόβον ,
sections.translation: you fear a tender child like this ! I do not commend the fear of one who fears but never yet has reasoned out the cause .

sections.label: Section 1168
sections.original: εἰ μὲν γὰρ ἔθανες πρὸ πόλεως , ἥβης τυχὼν
sections.translation: Ah ! my beloved , yours is a piteous death indeed ! If you had died for your city , when you had tasted of the sweets of manhood , of marriage , and of godlike power over others ,

sections.label: Section 1170
sections.original: μακάριος ἦσθʼ ἄν , εἴ τι τῶνδε μακάριον ·
sections.translation: then were you blessed , if anything here is blessed . But now after one glimpse , one dream of them , you know them no more , my child , and have no joy of them , though heir to all . Ah , poor child ! how sadly have your own father ’ s walls , those towers that Loxias reared , shorn from your head

sections.label: Section 1175
sections.original: ὃν πόλλʼ ἐκήπευσʼ ἡ τεκοῦσα βόστρυχον
sections.translation: the locks your mother fondled , and so often caressed , from which through fractured bones the face of murder grins — briefly to dismiss my shocking theme . O hands , how sweet the likeness you retain of his father , and yet you lie Iimp in your sockets before me !

sections.label: Section 1180
sections.original: ὦ πολλὰ κόμπους ἐκβαλὸν φίλον στόμα ,
sections.translation: Dear mouth , so often full of words of pride , death has closed you , and you have not kept the promise you made , when nestling in my robe , Ah , mother , many a lock of my hair I will cut off for you , and to your tomb will lead my troops of friends , taking a fond farewell of you .

sections.label: Section 1185
sections.original: σὺ δʼ οὐκ ἔμʼ , ἀλλʼ ἐγὼ σὲ τὸν νεώτερον ,
sections.translation: But now I am not to be buried by you , but you , the younger one , a wretched corpse , are buried by me , on whom old age has come with loss of home and children . Ah me , those kisses numberless , the nurture that I gave to you , those sleepless nights — they all are lost ! What shall the bard inscribe upon your tomb about you ?

sections.label: Section 1190
sections.original: Τὸν παῖδα τόνδʼ ἔκτειναν Ἀργεῖοί ποτε
sections.translation: Argives once for fear of him slew this child ? Foul shame should that inscription be to Hellas . O child , though you have no part in all your father ’ s wealth , yet shall you have his brazen shield in which to find a tomb . Ah ! shield that kept safe the comely arm of Hector ,

sections.label: Section 1195
sections.translation: now have you lost your valiant keeper ! How fair upon your handle lies his imprint , and on the rim that circles around are marks of sweat , that trickled often from Hector ’ s brow as he pressed it against his beard in battle ’ s stress .

sections.label: Section 1200
sections.translation: Come , bring forth , from such store as you have , adornment for the hapless dead , for fortune gives no chance now for lovely offerings ; yet of such as I possess , you shall receive these gifts . He is a foolish mortal who thinks his luck secure and so rejoices ; for fortune , like a madman in her moods ,

sections.label: Section 1205
sections.translation: springs towards this man , then towards that ; and no one ever experiences the same unchanging luck .

sections.label: Section 1207
sections.original: καὶ μὴν πρόχειρον αἵδε σοι σκυλευμάτων
sections.translation: Look ! all is ready and they are bringing at your bidding from the spoils of Troy adornment to put upon the dead .

sections.label: Section 1209
sections.original: ὦ τέκνον , οὐχ ἵπποισι νικήσαντά σε
sections.translation: Ah ! my child , it is not as victor over your comrades

sections.label: Section 1210
sections.original: οὐδʼ ἥλικας τόξοισιν , οὓς Φρύγες νόμους
sections.translation: with horse or bow — customs Troy esteems , without pursuing them to excess — that Hector ’ s mother decks you now with ornaments from the store that once was yours , though now Helen , whom the gods abhor , has bereft you of your own , yes , and robbed you

sections.label: Section 1215
sections.translation: of your life and caused your house to perish root and branch .

sections.label: Section 1216
sections.translation: Woe ! thrice woe ! my heart is touched , and you the cause , my mighty prince in days now passed !

sections.label: Section 1218
sections.original: ἃ δʼ ἐν γάμοισι χρῆν σε προσθέσθαι χροῒ
sections.translation: About your body now I swathe this Phrygian robe of honor , which should have clad you on your marriage-day ,

sections.label: Section 1220
sections.translation: wedded to the noblest of Asia ’ s daughters . You too , dear shield of Hector , victorious parent of countless triumphs past , accept your crown , for though you share the dead child ’ s tomb , death cannot touch you ; for you merit honors far beyond those arms [*]

sections.label: Section 1225
sections.original: κακοῦ τʼ Ὀδυσσέως ἄξιον τιμᾶν ὅπλα .
sections.translation: that the crafty villain Odysseus won .

sections.label: Section 1226
sections.translation: Alas ! alas ! you , O child , shall earth take to her breast , a cause for bitter weeping . Mourn , you mother !

sections.label: Section 1229b
sections.translation: Alas !

sections.label: Section 1230
sections.translation: Wail for the dead .

sections.label: Section 1230b
sections.translation: Woe is me !

sections.label: Section 1231
sections.translation: Woe indeed for your unending sorrow !

sections.label: Section 1232
sections.original: τελαμῶσιν ἕλκη τὰ μὲν ἐγώ σʼ ἰάσομαι ,
sections.translation: Your wounds in part I will bind up with bandages , a wretched healer in name alone , without reality ; but for the rest your father must look to that among the dead .

sections.label: Section 1235
sections.translation: Smite , oh smite upon your head with frequent blow of hand . Woe is me !

sections.label: Section 1238
sections.translation: My kind , good friends —

sections.label: Section 1239
sections.original: Ἑκάβη , σὰς ἔνεπε · τίνα θροεῖς αὐδάν ;
sections.translation: Speak out , Hecuba , the word that was on your lips .

sections.label: Section 1240
sections.original: οὐκ ἦν ἄρʼ ἐν θεοῖσι πλὴν οὑμοὶ πόνοι
sections.translation: It seems the only things that heaven concerns itself about are my troubles and Troy hateful in their eyes above all other cities . In vain did we sacrifice to them . But if the god had not caught us in his grip and plunged us headlong beneath the earth , we should have been unheard of , and not ever sung in Muses ’ songs ,

sections.label: Section 1245
sections.translation: furnishing to bards of after-days a subject for their minstrelsy . Go , bury now in his poor tomb the dead , wreathed all duly as befits a corpse . And yet I think it makes little difference to the dead , if they get a gorgeous funeral ;

sections.label: Section 1250
sections.original: κενὸν δὲ γαύρωμʼ ἐστὶ τῶν ζώντων τόδε .
sections.translation: but this is a cause of idle pride to the living . The corpse is carried off to burial .

sections.label: Section 1251
sections.translation: Alas ! for your unhappy mother , who over your corpse has closed the high hopes of her life ! Born of a noble stock , counted most happy in your lot ,

sections.label: Section 1255
sections.translation: ah ! what a tragic death is yours ! Ha ! who are those I see on yonder pinnacles darting to and fro with flaming torches in their hands ? Some new calamity wiII soon alight on Troy .

sections.label: Section 1260
sections.translation: You captains whose allotted task it is to fire this town of Priam , to you I speak . No longer keep the fire-brand idle in your hands , but launch the flame , that when we have destroyed the city of Ilium we may set forth in gladness on our homeward voyage from Troy .

sections.label: Section 1265
sections.original: ὑμεῖς δʼ , ἵνʼ αὑτὸς λόγος ἔχῃ μορφὰς δύο ,
sections.translation: And you , you sons of Troy , to let my orders take at once a double form — start for the Achaean ships for your departure from the land , as soon as the leaders of the army blow loud and clear upon the trumpet . And you , unhappy grey-haired lady ,

sections.label: Section 1270
sections.translation: follow ; for here come servants from Odysseus to fetch you , for to him you are assigned by lot to be a slave far from your country .

sections.label: Section 1272
sections.original: οἲ ʼγὼ τάλαινα · τοῦτο δὴ τὸ λοίσθιον
sections.translation: Ah , woe is me ! This surely is the last , the utmost limit , of all my sorrows ; I go forth from my land ; my city is ablaze with flame .

sections.label: Section 1275
sections.original: ἀλλʼ , ὦ γεραιὲ πούς , ἐπίσπευσον μόλις ,
sections.translation: Yet , you aged foot , make one painful struggle to haste , that I may say a farewell to this wretched town . O Troy , that before had such a grand career among barbarian towns , soon will you be bereft of that splendid name . They are burning you , and leading us even now from our land
